Your task is to execute the scheduled task below as specified in that task's description. You are the scheduled worker, not the main agent: you cannot reach the user.

When the scheduled work is done, end the turn with a short final message — even if there is nothing to report, say so briefly (for example, "Nothing to report."), never an empty message. That message is how the turn ends here; there is no terminal tool to call in this phase, and a later resolution step decides what, if anything, reaches the user. Do not use the cron tool to mark this run complete, re-run it, or modify schedules.

[WORKER TASK]
job_id: iwsdk-daily-build
mode: task

[BODY]
## IWSDK Daily Build — Initial Planning & Build

This is the midnight kick-off job. You start a fresh IWSDK game build from scratch.

### Before anything else:

1. **Read the IWSDK dev skill** at `~/workspace/skills/iwsdk-dev/SKILL.md` — especially section **4b (Spatial UI / PanelUI)**.
2. **Check the build log** at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md` — DO NOT repeat any concept already listed there.

### Status file protocol:

You MUST maintain `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/current-build-status.json` throughout.

**At the start**, write:
```json
{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "date": "YYYY-MM-DD",
  "game_name": "<game-name>",
  "game_dir": "/home/hatch/workspace/<game-name>",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>",
  "pages_url": "https://ellyz2426.github.io/<game-name>/",
  "build_start_utc": "<ISO timestamp>",
  "target_hours": 6,
  "total_build_minutes": 0,
  "status": "building",
  "rounds": [],
  "features_completed": [],
  "last_updated_utc": "<ISO timestamp>"
}
```

**When your build round ends**, update the status file:
- Set `status` to `"waiting_continuation"` (if under 6h) or `"complete"` (if ≥6h)
- Append to `rounds` array: `{"round": N, "start_utc": "...", "end_utc": "...", "minutes": N, "files": N, "lines": N, "features_added": [...]}`
- Update `total_build_minutes` (sum of all rounds)
- Update `features_completed` with everything built so far
- Set `last_updated_utc`

### ⚠️ CRITICAL: Spatial UI Only — NO HTML Overlays

**ALL game UI MUST use IWSDK's built-in PanelUI system. Do NOT use HTML DOM elements for any game UI.**

This is a hard gate. HTML `<div>`, `<canvas>`, `document.createElement`, `innerHTML`, or any DOM overlay for menus, HUDs, scorecards, settings, notifications, or any other game UI is **forbidden**. HTML overlays are invisible in XR and are an anti-pattern.

Instead, use:
- **`.uikitml` templates** in a `ui/` directory, compiled by `@iwsdk/vite-plugin-uikitml`
- **`PanelUI` component** to attach compiled UI panels to entities
- **`Follower` component** with `world.player.head` target for head-locked HUDs in XR
- **`ScreenSpace` component** for browser-mode overlays that auto-return to world-space in XR
- **World-space panels** for in-scene menus, leaderboards, settings boards

Read the IWSDK dev skill section **4b** for full API details, examples, and patterns.

### ⚠️ CRITICAL: XR Verification Gate

**You MUST verify all UI and interactions work in XR BEFORE expanding features or adding content.**

Build order for every game:
1. Core gameplay loop + basic XR controller input
2. All UI as PanelUI spatial panels (menus, HUD, scorecards, settings)
3. **VERIFY in XR:** Use IWSDK CLI tools (`npx iwsdk xr`, `npx iwsdk ecs pause/step/snapshot`) to confirm:
   - All panels render and are readable in XR
   - XR controller laser pointer can interact with all buttons/menus
   - HUD panels follow the player's head correctly
   - No HTML overlays remain
4. ONLY THEN expand with additional features, content, modes, polish

If XR verification fails, fix the issues before adding anything else.

### Build instructions:

1. **Research and brainstorm** a new game concept. Be creative.

2. **Build it** in `~/workspace/<game-name>/`:
   - Use the latest IWSDK (currently 0.4.1+)
   - Holodeck aesthetic (clean, futuristic, neon wireframe, luminous)
   - **ALL UI via PanelUI** — `.uikitml` templates, `Follower` for HUDs, `ScreenSpace` for overlays. Zero HTML DOM UI.
   - **XR controller support is mandatory** — trigger to fire/interact, grip for grab, pointer for aiming/menus. Must work end-to-end with VR controllers only. Use IWSDK's InputComponent API and action system.
   - Support both VR AND browser-first mode with dual runtime (`xr: { offer: 'once' }` + `browserControls: true`)
   - Include audio (procedural sound effects, music)
   - Full gameplay loop with scoring, progression, polish

3. **Create GitHub repo** under `ellyz2426`:
   ```
   gh repo create ellyz2426/<game-name> --public --source=. --push --description "<description>"
   ```

4. **Deploy to GitHub Pages**:
   ```
   npm run build
   PROJECT="$PWD"
   cd /tmp && rm -rf gh-pages-deploy && mkdir gh-pages-deploy && cd gh-pages-deploy
   git init && cp -R "$PROJECT/dist/." .
   git add -A && git commit -m "Deploy"
   git push --force "https://github.com/ellyz2426/<game-name>.git" HEAD:gh-pages
   ```
   Then configure Pages:
   ```
   gh api repos/ellyz2426/<game-name>/pages -X PUT -f "build_type=legacy" -F "source[branch]=gh-pages" -F "source[path]=/"
   ```
   Note: GitHub token does NOT have `workflow` scope.

5. **Push intermediate commits** to main throughout the build, and redeploy to gh-pages at major milestones.

6. **When your round ends**, update the status file, update the build log at `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/BUILD_LOG.md`, and write to `~/workspace/iwsdk-daily-builds/latest-build-summary.md`.

7. **Capture build journal** — Copy your raw session JSONL from `~/agents/` into `.agent/journal/` in the game repo (NOT `.hatch`). Commit and push.

Hourly check-in jobs will monitor your status file. If you stop early, they will continue the build.

[RUN CONTEXT]

The ring physics involves torus flight with gravity, angular momentum spin, peg bounce reflections, and encirclement detection based on 2D proximity + height + descent velocity.

**Concept:** Holodeck VR slicing game -- first slicing/cutting genre in the portfolio. Wield dual neon energy blades (one per VR controller) to slice through flying wireframe objects mid-flight. Objects launch from below in parabolic arcs, player swings controllers to cut them. Speed-based slice detection via line-segment/sphere intersection. Objects split into two glowing halves that drift apart with physics. Completely unique mechanic -- dual-hand blade tracking with velocity-gated cutting -- distinct from all prior hit/throw/aim games.

**Key Work:**
- Full IWSDK 0.4.1 dual-runtime (VR + browser) with `xr: { offer: 'once' }` + `canvasPointerEvents`
- Dual energy blade system: blade meshes (shaft + glow cylinder + handle + tip sphere) attached to each VR grip space. In browser, single blade follows mouse position raycasted to z=-1.8 plane
- Speed-based slice detection: each frame tracks blade tip positions (previous + current), computes tip velocity. If speed > 2.0 m/s, performs line-segment/bounding-sphere intersection test against all active objects. Uses quadratic discriminant for precise intersection
- 6 object types with distinct Three.js geometries and scoring:
  - Cube (BoxGeometry, cyan, 100pts)
  - Sphere (SphereGeometry, green, 150pts)
  - Diamond (OctahedronGeometry, magenta, 200pts)
  - Star (ConeGeometry, gold, 300pts)
  - Bomb (IcosahedronGeometry, red, -500pts, lose life)
  - Freeze (TorusKnotGeometry, blue, 50pts, 5s slow-motion)
- Object split effect: on slice, original hides, two half-meshes spawn offset along slice normal, drift apart with velocity + rotation + gravity + opacity fade over 1.2s
- 6 game modes:
  - Classic (10 waves, 3 lives, progressive difficulty)
  - Zen (no bombs, no pressure, endless flow)
  - Time Attack (60 seconds, maximize score)
  - Survival (increasing speed, 3 lives)
  - Frenzy (30s rapid fire, no bombs)

  apps, when working with PanelUI/uikitml, ECS systems, physics, the iwsdk CLI,
  or when the user mentions iwsdk, IWSDK, @iwsdk, WebXR, or `npx iwsdk`.

# IWSDK Development Guide

Targets the IWSDK **0.4.x** line · [iwsdk.dev](https://iwsdk.dev) · built on Three.js (super-three) + an ECS.

This skill is phase-based. Find the phase you're in below, read its terse highlights here,
and **load the one matching `references/` file** for depth. The correctness rules in the next
section are always in force, in every phase.

## CLI-first operating model

`npx iwsdk ...` is the primary control surface in cloud/headless harnesses — for setup, dev
server, screenshots, ECS/XR inspection, and **reference lookup**. Prefer it over MCP tools,
which are often unwired in cloud harnesses; do not rewrite CLI examples into MCP-only flows.

**Ground your API knowledge in the reference CLI instead of guessing.** `@iwsdk/reference` is a
semantic search over the real SDK (`reference search` / `api` / `systems` / `components` /
`examples`). It needs a **one-time warmup** that downloads a corpus + model into cache:

```bash
npx iwsdk reference warmup     # one-time, ~120MB into cache; run it during scaffold (Phase 0)
npx iwsdk reference status      # confirm ready
npx iwsdk reference api    --input-json '{"name":"World.create"}'
npx iwsdk reference search --input-json '{"query":"how to register a per-frame system","limit":5}'
```

> **If a reference command errors as not-ready / cache-miss / corpus-missing, RUN
> `npx iwsdk reference warmup` and retry — do NOT give up and guess the API.** Warmup is cheap
> and pays for itself: it is your ground truth for any "what's the real method/component?"
> question and the antidote to the `(world as any)` / wrong-API guessing that breaks builds.
> (See `references/cli-reference.md` for warmup recovery, and `references/api-quick-reference.md`
> for the typed surface at a glance.)

## The 7 build phases

| Phase                       | You are…                                     | Load                                   |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| 0 · Scaffold & First Render | creating the app + getting a frame to render | `references/01-scaffold-and-render.md` |
| 1 · World & the Game Loop   | calling `World.create`, standing up the loop | `references/02-world-and-loop.md`      |
| 2 · Gameplay Subsystems     | three imports, physics, audio, input, coords | `references/03-subsystems.md`          |
| 3 · Spatial UI (PanelUI)    | authoring + wiring uikitml panels            | `references/04-spatial-ui.md`          |
| 4 · Verify (continuous)     | proving it renders, ticks, responds          | `references/05-verify-runtime.md`      |
| 5 · Build & Deploy          | deploying — Hatch Space (default), GH Pages opt-in | `references/06-build-and-deploy.md`    |
| 6 · Iterate                 | re-verifying + extending across rounds       | `references/07-iterate.md`             |

## Non-negotiable correctness rules (always in force)

These six prevent the most expensive failures. Each is _decided early but surfaces late_, so
internalize them up front. Terse here; full treatment in the tagged reference.

1. **Import every Three.js class from `@iwsdk/core`, never from `three`.** The runtime is
   super-three; importing from `three` (or `import * as THREE`) drifts types and `instanceof`.
   IWSDK apps are ESM-only — no `require`. _[Phase 2 → 03-subsystems.md]_
2. **Per-frame logic MUST be an ECS System** — `class X extends createSystem({...})` with
   `update(delta, time)`, registered via `world.registerSystem(X)`. `world.onUpdate`,
   `world.ecs.registerSystem`, `renderer.setAnimationLoop`, and raw `requestAnimationFrame`
   do **not** tick the loop and silently ship a dead game. _[Phase 1 → 02-world-and-loop.md]_
3. **`World` is fully typed — never `(world as any)`.** `world.camera`, `world.scene`,
   `world.renderer`, `world.input`, `world.player`, `world.registerSystem` all exist. A TS error
   here is a real bug, not a framework quirk. _[Phase 1 → 02-world-and-loop.md]_
4. **PanelUI loads async, is uikit (not DOM), and needs relative config paths.** Wire handlers
   in the query `'qualify'` event (not `setTimeout`); mutate with `setProperties` (not
   `textContent`/`onclick`); author `config: './ui/x.json'` relative (root-absolute breaks deploy: GitHub 404s, and the Space daemon serves only `assets/`).
   _[Phase 3 author → 04-spatial-ui.md · Phase 5 bites → 06-build-and-deploy.md]_
5. **`tsc --noEmit` + a rendered screenshot is the real verification gate.** `vite build` does
   not type-check; a green build is **not** verification. Re-run the gate every milestone.
   _[Phase 4 → 05-verify-runtime.md]_
6. **Use the real subsystem APIs, not hand-rolled substitutes.** Physics needs `PhysicsShape` +
   `PhysicsBody` (don't write an integrator); audio uses `AudioSource` / `AudioUtils` (not raw
   Web Audio). _[Phase 2 → 03-subsystems.md]_

When unsure of any API, confirm it with `npx iwsdk reference api|systems|components` (warm it up
first) rather than casting to `any`.

## Phase highlights

### Phase 0 — Scaffold & First Render → `references/01-scaffold-and-render.md`

- Check `node -v` ≥ 20.19 **before** scaffolding (Vite 7 throws `crypto.hash is not a function`
  on Node 18; `dev:runtime` spawns a child `vite` that reverts to the default Node — make 20/22
  the persistent default, don't drop it in `/tmp`).
- Scaffold **fresh** with `npx @iwsdk/create@latest … --no-metaspatial`; infer flags from the
  request. Never clone-and-rename a prior game (drags stale assets/config/anti-patterns).
- `npm run dev` == `npx iwsdk dev up`; read `localUrl` (`https://127.0.0.1:8081/`) from
  `dev status`. SwiftShader auto-detects in 0.4.x — no `node_modules` patching.
- **Warm up the reference CLI now:** `npx iwsdk reference warmup` (so API lookups work later).
- Behind a proxy / on Node-18 / on a Playwright `chromium_headless_shell-1208` mismatch → load
  the reference.

### Phase 1 — World & the Game Loop → `references/02-world-and-loop.md`

- `await World.create(container, {...})`; options are typed (don't cast). Rules **2 and 3** live
  here — this is the costly correctness fork.
- After `World.create`, register systems: `world.registerSystem(GameSystem)`. The loop is
  already running; never start your own.

### Phase 2 — Gameplay Subsystems → `references/03-subsystems.md`

- Three imports (rule 1), physics components (rule 6), `AudioSource`/`AudioUtils` (rule 6),
  XR controller + keyboard input, coordinates. One file, jump via its `## Contents`.
- Input gamepad APIs take **string ids** (`InputComponent.Trigger`) and `getAxesValues` returns
  `{x,y}` — a number id or array index silently returns `undefined`.

### Phase 3 — Spatial UI (PanelUI / uikitml) → `references/04-spatial-ui.md`

- Author `ui/*.uikitml`; the `compileUIKit()` vite plugin emits `public/ui/*.json` (no CLI to
  compile). Reference the **compiled** path, **relative** (rule 4).
- Loads async → wire in the `'qualify'` event; elements are uikit → `setProperties`. ASCII-only
  glyphs; no multi-value `padding`/`margin` shorthand.

### Phase 4 — Verify in the Live Runtime → `references/05-verify-runtime.md`

- Rule 5. `npx tsc --noEmit` → `dev up` → `browser screenshot` → `browser logs` (zero errors) →
  `ecs systems` (your systems are registered = the loop is real). Re-run every milestone.
- `ecs pause`/`step` to freeze a play frame for a stable screenshot.

### Phase 5 — Build & Deploy → `references/06-build-and-deploy.md`

- `npm run build`, then **deploy as a Hatch Space by default**: `create_space` (registers it) →
  rewrite `index.html` (both `data-generated-space-root=""` + `data-generated-space-bootstrap=""`
  markers, drop the SDK bundle) → move every asset under `assets/` → patch runtime paths to
  `./assets/…` → `share share-space --slug <slug>`. Rebuild before publishing (stale-artifact trap).
- **GitHub Pages is opt-in** — only when the user explicitly asks (or needs VM-independent
  hosting, since a `local` Space needs the VM running): `npm run build && npx gh-pages -d dist`.
- The #1 deploy break: the Space daemon serves **only `assets/`**, so any runtime-fetched path
  (PanelUI `config`, gltf/audio/textures) 401s unless it resolves under `./assets/` — and the same
  root-absolute `config: '/ui/…'` 404s on a GitHub subpath. Author configs relative.

### Phase 6 — Iterate → `references/07-iterate.md`

- Re-run the full Phase 4 gate **every** round against _this_ build; never reuse a prior round's
  counts/claims. Recover an unresponsive runtime; re-sync adapters after dependency changes.

## When to load references (route by what you're doing)

| If you're… / you hit…                                                  | Load                                |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| "nothing is ticking / `ecs systems` is empty / dead loop"              | `references/02-world-and-loop.md`   |
| "a panel won't appear or bind"                                         | `references/04-spatial-ui.md`       |
| "401/404 on panels or assets **after deploy**" (Space or Pages)        | `references/06-build-and-deploy.md` |
| "build passed — is it done?"                                           | `references/05-verify-runtime.md`   |
| confirming a real member/signature (instead of casting to `any`)       | `references/api-quick-reference.md` |
| any `iwsdk` command, warmup recovery, or "does this subcommand exist?" | `references/cli-reference.md`       |
| a raw error string you can't place                                     | `references/troubleshooting.md`     |

Run the bundled gates with `bash scripts/preflight-checks.sh` (non-ASCII glyphs, Yoga shorthand,
root-absolute UI configs, CJS idioms, uikitml→json coverage) before deploying.

## Top friction points (most-wasteful mistakes, by phase)

1. Shipping a **no-op game loop** via raw rAF / `world.onUpdate` / `world.ecs.registerSystem` — _P1_.
2. Reinventing **PanelUI readiness** with `setTimeout` instead of the `'qualify'` event — _P3_.
3. **Assets the Space daemon won't serve** (everything fetched at runtime must live under `assets/`) / root-absolute UI config that 404s on a GitHub subpath — _P3/P5_.
4. Treating a green `vite build` as **verification** (it doesn't type-check) — _P4_.
5. Casting `(world as any)` / guessing APIs instead of warming up the **reference CLI** — _all phases_.
6. Building on **Node 18** (Vite 7 `crypto.hash`) or cloning a prior game as a scaffold — _P0_.

item.thinking:
Let me review the genres already covered:

From the completed game names:
VR Pong, Galaga (shmup), Neon Beats (rhythm), Neon Bastion (tower defense?), Holo Golf, Astro Bowl (bowling), Void Archer (archery), Phantom Pool (pool/billiards), Neon Pinball, Neon Darts, Neon Hockey (air hockey), Neon Hoops (basketball), Neon Boxing, Neon Blade (sword/combat), Neon Drift (racing), Neon Smash (badminton?), Neon Marble (marble maze), Neon Slugger (baseball), Neon Curling, Neon Volley (volleyball), Neon Paddle (table tennis), Neon Breaker (breakout), Neon Skee (skee-ball), Neon Claw (crane machine), Neon Bubble (bubble shooter), Neon Fishing, Neon Cannon (artillery), Neon Whack (whack-a-mole), Neon Memory (Simon Says), Neon Slicer (fruit ninja), Neon Drop (columns/puyo puyo), Neon Blocks (tetris), Neon Toss (ring toss), Neon Launch (launch game), Neon Orbit (orbital mechanics?), Neon Sprint (endless runner), Neon Stack (stacking), Neon Lanes (bowling), Neon Gems (match-3), Neon Mines (minesweeper), Neon Connect (connect four), Neon Sudoku, Neon Blackjack, Neon Cipher (puzzle game).

Continuing with more game concepts: word puzzles like Neon Cipher, pipe routing challenges, laser reflection mechanics, classic card games like Solitaire, spatial puzzle games like Sokoban and Mahjong, retro arcade titles like Snake and Pac-Man, modern puzzle hits like 2048 and Picross, strategy games ranging from Chess and Checkers to Backgammon and Mancala, and casino-style games including Roulette, Slots, and Craps. Also planning arcade shooters like Asteroids and Tempest, plus tower defense and missile command variants.

Finishing out the list with word search, helicopter cave flyers, trivia quizzes, light cycle games, FPS wave defense, poker, auto-runner platformers, foosball, territory capture mechanics, Bomberman-style games, Peggle physics puzzles, and lunar lander simulations — that brings the total to over 80 different game genres covered.

Now I'm identifying what's still missing: traditional side-scrolling platformers like Mario, tower-building or Jenga-style stacking games, rhythm and dance games, and word guessing games like Hangman.

Looking at the remaining possibilities: typing games, jigsaw and sliding puzzles, Tangram, dominoes, card games like Cribbage and Spades, Tower of Hanoi, crosswords, color-matching or Lights Out mechanics, mini golf variations, shuffleboard, cornhole, classic Space Invaders, Lemmings-style puzzle platformers, board games like Go and Hex, and Dots and Boxes.

Now expanding into more arcade classics like Centipede, Defender, Galaxian, Dig Dug, Q*bert, Joust, and Gauntlet, plus additional game types like side-scrolling beat 'em ups, rhythm games in the Guitar Hero or Dance Dance Revolution style, and various target sports—pool, snooker, bocce, lawn darts, archery, knife and axe throwing, horseshoe toss, and obstacle course or laser tag mechanics.

Brainstorming some fresh concepts now: stealth action with shuriken throwing and sensor dodging, gravity manipulation puzzles, tetherball, block stacking like Jenga, logic puzzles, farming simulations, wire untangling challenges, and domino chain reactions.

Continuing through more possibilities—bingo, crossword puzzles, tangram shape puzzles, zen garden sandbox experiences, shuffleboard sports—but many of these either already exist in some form or feel derivative. Landing on **Neon Invader VR** as a fresh take on the classic Space Invaders formula.

Actually, reconsidering since Galaga already covers vertical space shooters. **Neon Shuffle VR** as a shuffleboard game with sliding mechanics and precision scoring feels genuinely unique, or **Neon Towers VR** as a Jenga-style physics puzzle where you carefully extract blocks without toppling the structure. Trying to figure out which would be the most engaging and visually compelling experience.

Looking back at the failed attempts — Neon Tower, Neon Plinko, Neon Maze, and Neon Bounce — some of these could be worth revisiting. Plinko specifically interests me because it's distinct from the peggle-style bouncing in Neon Peg VR; it's about dropping a disc from the top and letting it cascade through pegs into scoring slots, like the Price is Right board.

I'm scanning through untouched genres to find something fresh and compelling. There's a lot of potential here — shuffleboard, block tower puzzles, Lights Out, tangrams, dominoes, and others. But Neon Plinko keeps pulling me back as the strongest choice for a retry.

The core appeal is clear: watching a disc cascade down through neon pegs with physics-driven randomness, landing in scored slots at the bottom. It's visually satisfying and mechanically distinct from what we've built before. Though I'm realizing Neon Peg VR is already Peggle-style peg bouncing, so Plinko would be too similar. I should pivot toward something more unique instead — Neon Shuffle VR with shuffleboard mechanics feels like the better direction.

Actually, I'm reconsidering what would be most visually compelling and VR-friendly. Neon Climb VR with procedurally generated walls and hand-based grabbing mechanics would leverage the controllers really well, but the climbing physics would be complex to get right without actual VR testing. So I'm settling on Neon Hanoi VR — the Tower of Hanoi puzzle translated into a neon VR experience.

Though thinking about it more, Tower of Hanoi might be too minimal for a full game experience. What I really want is something that hasn't been done before and would make a genuinely great game. Neon Klax VR could be perfect — it's a falling-tile catching game where you catch tiles on a paddle and flip them into a bin to create matching patterns. It combines reflex-based catching with strategic placement, blending mechanics that don't exist in other neon games I've considered.

But I'm also drawn to a first-person maze runner. The key difference from Neon Marble is that you'd actually be walking through the maze from inside it rather than tilting a board to guide a marble through it. I could use procedural generation with depth-first search, add fog of war, collectibles, a timer, minimap, and compass features across different maze sizes.

Actually, let me reconsider — I'm thinking about **Neon Shuffle VR** instead. Shuffleboard has clean mechanics (sliding pucks to score), works naturally in VR with controller aiming, and the neon aesthetic would look stunning on a polished table with glowing scoring zones. It's a completely fresh genre for this project.

But wait, I'm second-guessing myself again. Maybe something more ambitious like **Neon Heist VR** — a stealth puzzle game navigating laser grids, timing moves past sweeping beams, using mirrors to redirect them, hacking terminals. Though that might be overcomplicating things for the first round. I should stick with something I can execute cleanly.

Actually, shuffleboard could work well as **Neon Shuffle VR** with solid physics, multiple difficulty levels, and various game modes. But I'm realizing it shares too much DNA with curling since both are about precision sliding mechanics toward targets. I need something genuinely different.

Let me pivot to **Neon Tower VR** instead — a block-stacking game where pieces swing back and forth and you time your drops to stack them higher, with overhanging sections getting trimmed. It's simple and addictive, perfect for VR. Or actually, for build #95 I want to go stronger with **Neon Platformer VR** — a traditional 2.5D side-scrolling platformer where you control a character jumping across platforms and collecting gems, viewed from a third-person perspective. This is different from the auto-runner approach and feels like a solid, classic game type for VR.

Wait, that might be too similar to what I've already done. Let me consider **Neon Hexa VR** instead — a hexagonal puzzle game where you place hex-shaped pieces on a hex grid to complete lines and clear them, like Tetris but with a genuinely different mechanic. Though that might blend too much with other puzzle games. So I'm leaning toward **Neon Defend VR** — a tower defense or base defense game where enemies approach from all directions and you aim and shoot them down, which would be distinct from the FPS wave defense style I've already explored.

Actually, both of those feel like they're overlapping with existing titles in the portfolio. Let me look at what genres I haven't covered yet — dungeon crawlers, tower stacking, Plinko, first-person mazes, shuffleboard, word games like Hangman and Crossword, Tangram, and Jigsaw puzzles are all still open.

Going through other classic game types: Lights Out could work as a logic puzzle. That's a genuinely new genre — a grid-based puzzle where tapping tiles toggles them and their adjacent neighbors, with the goal of turning all lights off. I could generate hundreds of procedurally created puzzles across different grid sizes, add a minimum-moves challenge mode, and include hints for players who get stuck.

But I'm second-guessing myself — Lights Out might be too niche and not visually exciting enough. Juggling in VR crossed my mind as an alternative, where you'd use controllers to toss and catch objects with increasingly difficult patterns, though that would be hard to test without actual VR hardware.

I need to commit to something that's both genuinely new and visually striking.

Neon Towers VR feels like the right call — it's a precision stacking game inspired by arcade Stacker, where blocks oscillate and you time your button press to stack them perfectly. Each layer gets trimmed if it overhangs, creating a growing tower of neon blocks. It's got that one-more-try addictiveness, simple timing mechanics, and stunning visuals, though I'm realizing something similar might already exist.

Let me check what Neon Stack VR actually is — looks like it's a full stacking game with 3,145 lines of code and multiple modes, so there's definitely overlap. That rules out Tower Stacking as a concept.

I'm pivoting to Neon Tangram VR instead — a spatial puzzle where you grab and rotate seven geometric pieces in 3D space to match target shapes. It's a completely new genre for the portfolio, visually gorgeous with neon glowing pieces, and has hundreds of possible puzzles to solve. It appeals to both meditative and challenging gameplay styles, and VR is perfect for manipulating 3D shapes.

Actually, wait — I'm reconsidering Neon Invade VR as a Space Invaders game. While Galaga already exists in the portfolio, Space Invaders is fundamentally different: enemies march in formation and drop down, you're a stationary turret with destructible shields, and there's a mystery ship that crosses the top. The escalating panic as invaders get closer and faster is iconic and distinct from Galaga's diving enemies and capture mechanics. This feels like the stronger choice — it's instantly recognizable, genuinely different from what's already there, and the neon holodeck aesthetic would look incredible. Going with Neon Invade VR as build #95. A bouncing ball game where you control a paddle to direct an auto-bouncing ball — either horizontally like Breakout or vertically like Doodle Jump. But I'm not sure what the original #43 was supposed to be, so I'll skip the retry and go with something fresh instead.

**Neon Turret VR** appeals to me — a 360° turret defense game where you're locked in place shooting waves of enemies from all directions, with different enemy types and weapon upgrades. Though that feels redundant with Neon Arena and Neon Command already covering wave defense and missile defense.

So I'm settling on **Neon Tower VR**, a retry of the failed build #27.

Actually, I'm pivoting this to a Jenga-style block-removal game instead. You pull blocks from a physics-based tower without toppling it — in VR you reach in and carefully slide blocks out with your controller, and in the browser you click and drag to extract them. The tower sways and leans as blocks come out, and you score points for each successful removal before it inevitably collapses. I can add different game modes like classic rules, speed challenges, and precision variants with weighted blocks.

The tension of carefully extracting a block in VR while feeling the tower wobble would be genuinely compelling. I'm calling it **Neon Tower VR** — it's a retry of a failed build, and it differentiates from the existing stacking games by being extraction-focused instead. Though I'm realizing the realistic Jenga physics with block-on-block friction and tipping calculations could get complex without a full physics engine, even though IWSDK has physics components available.

Let me step back and think about what actually works here. I need something that's a genuinely new genre, buildable in one round, visually striking, fun to play, and VR-friendly. **Neon Shuffle VR** keeps pulling at me — tabletop shuffleboard is distinct enough from curling (it's close-quarters on a polished table with puck-on-puck impacts rather than a large ice rink with sweeping). A neon shuffleboard table would look stunning. That's my final call: **Neon Shuffle VR** as a tabletop shuffleboard game.

Actually, I'm second-guessing myself one more time. The portfolio is already heavy on sports and arcade games — what if I went somewhere completely different? **Neon Conductor VR** could be a music/rhythm conductor game where I use VR controllers as batons to lead an orchestra, timing gestures to the beat and controlling tempo and instrument sections. It's novel but might be too complex without solid music assets. **Neon Paint VR** is another option — collaborative 3D painting with neon brushes, drawing challenges, Pictionary-style games — but that's too open-ended for a scored experience. I'm sticking with **Neon Shuffle**.

Wait, what about **Neon Crypt VR** — a dungeon crawler roguelike with randomly generated rooms, monster combat, loot, leveling, turn-based grid movement? That would be a massive genre shift and really showcase the SDK differently. But it's too complex for a first pass. I need something simple and polished.

**Neon Shuffle VR** is my final choice — tabletop shuffleboard. Time to scaffold, code, deploy, and update the status files. Actually, hold on — I keep gravitating toward sports games. What about something totally different like **Neon Hanoi VR** — the Tower of Hanoi puzzle?

I could flesh it out with multiple difficulty levels, timed challenges, move counters with par systems, star ratings, different tower styles, daily challenges, speed run modes, and smooth neon animations. But honestly, Tower of Hanoi alone might feel too thin as a standalone game.

Okay, I'm committing to **Neon Gravity VR** instead — a gravity manipulation puzzle game where you guide a neon sphere through obstacle-filled levels by switching gravity direction in four cardinal directions, navigating around walls and hazards to reach the goal, similar to VVVVVV or Gravity Guy but in VR.

The core loop is simple: the ball always falls in whichever direction gravity points, and you switch gravity to navigate. I can scale this with simple introductory levels up to mind-bending puzzles, add gems to collect, moving platforms, and the VR perspective makes spatial reasoning really compelling. This feels like the right choice.

Now I'm setting up the project with Node 20 and scaffolding the initial structure.

# Phase 3 — Spatial UI (PanelUI / uikitml)

PanelUI is the spatial-UI system. It is a **two-step authoring → runtime flow**, it
loads **asynchronously**, and its elements are **uikit components, not the DOM**.
These three facts cause most UI friction; each is covered below. (There is also a
deeper `iwsdk-ui` skill.)

## Contents

- [Compile pipeline (no CLI — it's a Vite plugin)](#compile-pipeline-no-cli--its-a-vite-plugin)
- [Loads asynchronously — gate on the `qualify` event, not `setTimeout`](#loads-asynchronously--gate-on-the-qualify-event-not-settimeout)
- [Runtime access — uikit components, NOT the DOM](#runtime-access--uikit-components-not-the-dom)
- [uikitml authoring rules](#uikitml-authoring-rules)
- [`PanelUISystem` config](#paneluisystem-config)
- [`config` paths must be relative](#config-paths-must-be-relative)

## Compile pipeline (no CLI — it's a Vite plugin)

You author `ui/*.uikitml`; the `@iwsdk/vite-plugin-uikitml` plugin (`compileUIKit()`
in `vite.config.ts`) compiles them to `public/ui/*.json` with an exact 1:1 name map.
`PanelUI` then references the **compiled JSON path**, never the `.uikitml` source.

There is **NO standalone compile command.** Do not run `iwsdk uikit compile`,
`iwsdk ui compile`, or `npx @iwsdk/vite-plugin-uikitml compile` — none exist
(`iwsdk` only has `adapter|dev|mcp|reference|runtime|status`; the plugin ships no
bin). Never hand-author the JSON.

```ts
// vite.config.ts — order matters: iwsdkDev BEFORE compileUIKit
plugins: [
  mkcert(),
  iwsdkDev({
    /* ... */
  }),
  compileUIKit({ sourceDir: 'ui', outputDir: 'public/ui', verbose: true }),
];
```

Compilation runs **only** inside Vite: at **dev** (initial compile + file watcher →
HMR reload) and at **build** (prints a `🎨 UIKitML Compilation Summary:` listing each
emitted JSON — this summary appears on **build only**, never during dev serve). It
`ensureDir`s the output, so no `mkdir -p public/ui` is needed.

- `[compile-uikitml] Source directory not found: ui` / `No .uikitml files found`
  are **benign** (verbose-only, no error) — they just mean you have no `ui/` yet.
  Do not abandon PanelUI over them; create `ui/<name>.uikitml`.
- `public/ui/*.json` is **build output** — treat it as generated. Some templates
  `.gitignore` it; others commit it. Either way, a missing/misnamed JSON **404s
  silently at runtime** (`loadPanel` does `await fetch(config)` and only
  `console.error`s a 404 — the build still passes and the panel just never appears).
  Symptoms live in `iwsdk browser logs`, not the terminal.

```bash
# Verify every .uikitml has a compiled JSON (any name printed = a panel that will 404):
comm -23 <(ls ui/*.uikitml | xargs -n1 basename | sed 's/\.uikitml$//' | sort) \
         <(ls public/ui/*.json 2>/dev/null | xargs -n1 basename | sed 's/\.json$//' | sort)
# When cloning/reusing a project, delete public/ui/ first so stale orphan JSON
# (a .json with no .uikitml source) can't ship dead UI.
```

## Loads asynchronously — gate on the `qualify` event, not `setTimeout`

`addComponent(PanelUI, { config })` loads the JSON asynchronously and attaches the
`PanelDocument` component (and the queryable `UIKitDocument`) **only after** the load
resolves. So immediately after `addComponent`, `getValue(PanelDocument, 'document')`
is `undefined` for the first frames.

**Do NOT** use `setTimeout(bind, 500/1500/3000)`, accumulated-delta timers, or
per-frame `tryBindUI()` poll loops. There is a deterministic ready signal: the ECS
query **`qualify` event** on a query that requires both `PanelUI` and `PanelDocument`.
It fires exactly once, the frame the document becomes available. This is the
official starter-template pattern:

```ts
import {
  createSystem,
  PanelUI,
  PanelDocument,
  UIKitDocument,
  UIKit,
  eq,
} from '@iwsdk/core';

export class GameUISystem extends createSystem({
  // qualifies only after the async document load completes;
  // scope per-config with `where` when you have multiple panels.
  menu: {
    required: [PanelUI, PanelDocument],
    where: [eq(PanelUI, 'config', './ui/menu.json')],
  },
}) {
  init() {
    this.queries.menu.subscribe('qualify', (entity) => {
      const doc = PanelDocument.data.document[entity.index] as UIKitDocument;
      if (!doc) return; // defensive; qualify implies attached

      const start = doc.getElementById('btn-start') as UIKit.Text;
      start?.addEventListener('click', () => this.startGame());
    });
  }
}

world.registerSystem(GameUISystem);
```

## Runtime access — uikit components, NOT the DOM

`getElementById` returns an untyped uikit `Component<any>`. Narrow it to the concrete
uikit element type and use the **typed uikit API** — do not cast to `any`.

```ts
const doc = entity.getValue(PanelDocument, 'document') as
  | UIKitDocument
  | undefined;

const label = doc?.getElementById('score') as UIKit.Text | undefined;
label?.setProperties({ text: '1200' }); // set text
label?.setProperties({ backgroundColor: '#ff8800', borderColor: '#664400' }); // style

const btn = doc?.getElementById('start') as UIKit.Text | undefined;
btn?.addEventListener('click', () => startGame()); // wire interaction
```

Gotchas (these cause the recurring `as any` and `TS2339` churn):

- These are uikit elements, **not** DOM. `.textContent`, `.innerText`, `.onclick`,
  `.style` do not exist — `tsc` throws `TS2339: Property 'text'/'onclick' does not
exist on type 'Component<any>'`.
- **Mutate via `setProperties({ ... })`.** Do not assign `el.text.value = x` or
  `el.backgroundColor = { value: x }` — those are private signals; they may work at
  runtime but are off the public type, which is exactly why agents reach for `as any`.
- Read computed values via `el.properties.value.<prop>`.
- Selectors: `#id` → `getElementById`, `.class` → `querySelector`. Set the element's
  `name` in uikitml to make it discoverable by id.

Typed helper (drop-in, no `as any`):

```ts
const getDoc = (e: Entity) =>
  e.getValue(PanelDocument, 'document') as UIKitDocument | undefined;
const setText = (e: Entity, id: string, text: string) =>
  (getDoc(e)?.getElementById(id) as UIKit.Text | undefined)?.setProperties({
    text,
  });
```

Canonical reference: `examples/audio/src/panel.ts`,
`examples/environment-raycast/src/panel.ts`.

## uikitml authoring rules

`.uikitml` compiles via `@pmndrs/uikitml` then renders through `@pmndrs/uikit`. The
compile step does **not** validate layout values or glyph coverage — both classes of
error escape the build and only surface at render time in XR. Catch them before
verifying:

**1. ASCII only in panel text** (and in runtime panel strings set from `.ts`). The
default font (Inter MSDF) has limited glyph coverage; emoji, `★ ☆`, `◀ ▶ ⬆`, em-dash
`—`, bullets `• ·`, box-drawing `─` emit `Missing glyph info for character …`
(console-only; build still succeeds) and render as blank/tofu in the headset.

| Want          | Use         |
| ------------- | ----------- |
| `—` (em-dash) | `-` or `--` |
| `← →`         | `<-` `->`   |
| `★ ⭐`        | `*`         |
| `• ·`         | `-` or `*`  |
| `▶ ◀`       | `>` `<`     |

```bash
# Flag any non-ASCII left in panel sources (automate this — manual sweeps miss glyphs):
grep -rnP '[^\x00-\x7F]' ui/*.uikitml src/**/*.ts
```

**2. No multi-value padding/margin shorthand.** `padding: 0.8 2;` or `margin: 0.3 0;`
is **not** supported. `compileUIKit` accepts it and writes JSON, but at render Yoga
throws `Invalid Yoga point: 0.8 2` — a late failure during XR verification, not at
build. Use a single value or explicit per-side props:

```css
/* BAD — throws "Invalid Yoga point" at render */
padding: 0.8 2;
/* GOOD */
padding: 1; /* single value */
padding-top: 0.8;
padding-bottom: 0.8;
padding-left: 2;
padding-right: 2;
```

```bash
grep -rnE '(padding|margin)[a-z-]*:[[:space:]]*[-0-9.]+[[:space:]]+[-0-9.]+' ui/*.uikitml
```

## `PanelUISystem` config

Only `kits` and `preferredColorScheme` are accepted by `PanelUISystem`
(`forwardHtmlEvents` was removed in 0.4.0).

## `config` paths must be relative

Author `config: './ui/x.json'` (relative), **never** `'/ui/x.json'` (root-absolute).
The runtime loads it with a raw `fetch(config)` that Vite `base` does not rewrite, so a
root-absolute path 404s on any GitHub subpath deploy — and on the **default Hatch Space
deploy** the daemon serves only `assets/`, so the path additionally has to be relocated
under `./assets/ui/` at deploy time. This is the single most common deploy break — see
06-build-and-deploy.md for the full treatment (Space relocation + the pre-deploy grep
gate). The scaffold and `examples/*` all author these relative (`./ui/welcome.json`);
keep that style from the start.

item.thinking:
Good, I have the PanelUI reference. Now let me build the complete game. I'll write the full source code for Neon Gravity VR.

Key design decisions:
- Gravity manipulation puzzle: ball falls in the direction of current gravity
- Switch gravity with arrow keys (browser) or thumbstick directions (VR)
- Grid-based levels with walls, hazards, gems, and goal
- Multiple level sizes and difficulties
- 8 game modes
- 40+ achievements
- 15+ PanelUI panels
- Procedural audio
- Holodeck aesthetic

The core mechanics revolve around a ball that always accelerates toward the current gravity direction—up, down, left, or right—with friction when it contacts surfaces. Players navigate grid-based levels by switching gravity with arrow keys or VR controls, avoiding spikes and walls while collecting gems and reaching the goal zone. Each cell in the level grid represents either empty space, walls, hazards, collectibles, the goal, or the player's starting position.

Writing the complete index.ts file and UI templates...

I'm also realizing the VR approach needs to be different from a traditional 2D gravity game. Since rotating the camera in VR causes motion sickness, the level itself stays fixed while the ball falls in whatever direction gravity points. The player observes from a stationary VR viewpoint, watching the action unfold on a floating holographic game board—essentially a neon grid puzzle suspended in front of them. This creates the perfect holodeck aesthetic with a glowing grid and the puzzle mechanics displayed on a vertical plane.

For the game board itself, I'm picturing a floating vertical plane with a glowing sphere as the ball, neon blocks for walls, and an arrow indicator showing the current gravity direction. The physics will be straightforward: the ball moves at constant speed in the gravity direction, stops when hitting walls, and changes direction when gravity switches. Gems get collected on contact, spikes are lethal, and reaching the goal zone completes the level.

Now I'm ready to build out the full implementation with all the game systems, level data, UI templates, and mechanics working together. The ball slides in the direction of gravity until it hits an obstacle, so the puzzle becomes finding the right sequence of gravity switches to navigate from start to goal while collecting gems and avoiding hazards. I'm structuring 24 levels across four difficulty tiers, starting with 8x8 grids for the tutorial and scaling up to 14x14 for expert levels, using a grid notation where dots are empty spaces, hashes are walls, S marks the start, G marks the goal, asterisks are gems, X marks hazards, and P represents paired portals.

I'm also adding special tile types like breakable walls, moving walls, anti-gravity tiles that reverse your current gravity direction, and gravity locks that force gravity in one direction for a few moves. For the physics, I'm going with a real-time model where the ball accelerates under gravity, bounces off walls with some damping, and comes to rest when touching a surface aligned with the current gravity direction—when you switch gravity, the ball starts falling in the new direction.

Now I'm building out the full game implementation with a compact level format using strings to define the grid layout, and I'll structure it with constants, level data, a game state manager with save persistence, audio, particles, and rendering.

Writing game code... all gems collected and finishing under par time. I'm mapping out eight different game modes ranging from a structured campaign with lives to more experimental options like procedurally generated levels and daily challenges, plus forty achievements tracking everything from level milestones to career totals. Now I'm thinking through the ball physics—when the player switches gravity, the ball needs to accelerate smoothly in that direction and stop cleanly against obstacles, creating that satisfying puzzle-solving rhythm.

For the 3D visuals, I'm picturing the game board as a floating square in front of the player with each cell rendered as a distinct 3D element: walls as solid cubes with wireframe outlines, gems as rotating octahedrons with glow effects, hazards as red spike cones, the goal as a pulsing ring, portals as spinning tori, and the ball itself as a glowing sphere with a motion trail. I'll add a directional arrow to show which way gravity's currently pulling.

Now I'm working out the board dimensions—keeping it consistently around 1.2 meters across by adjusting the cell size based on grid dimensions, then positioning the whole board about 2 meters in front of the player at eye level.

I'm ready to write the full game implementation as a comprehensive TypeScript file, making sure to follow all the IWSDK requirements like importing from the core library, using createSystem for game systems, registering with the world, and supporting both XR controllers and dual runtimes while avoiding any DOM-based UI.

{"content":"<container name="root" flexDirection="column" alignItems="center" gap="6" padding="12" backgroundColor="#000000cc" borderRadius="8" width="340">

  <container flexDirection="column" gap="4" width="300">
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#00ffcc">CONCEPT</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Switch gravity direction to slide the ball through neon mazes. Collect gems, avoid hazards, reach the goal!</text>
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#ffcc00">BROWSER CONTROLS</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Arrow Keys / WASD - Change gravity direction</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">R - Restart level</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">ESC / P - Pause</text>
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#ff8844">VR CONTROLS</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Right Thumbstick - Change gravity direction</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">B Button - Pause</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Trigger - Confirm / Menu interact</text>
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#44ff44">ELEMENTS</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#00ffcc">Cyan blocks - Walls (solid)</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#ffcc00">Yellow gems - Collect for bonus</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#ff4444">Red spikes - Hazards (kill)</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#44ff44">Green ring - Goal (reach it!)</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#ff66cc">Pink portals - Teleport in pairs</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#ff8844">Orange blocks - Breakable (one hit)</text>
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#ff66cc">SCORING</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Base 1000 + Gem bonus (200ea) + Move bonus (under par) + Time bonus</text>
    <text fontSize="11" fontWeight="bold" color="#aaaaff">8 GAME MODES</text>
    <text fontSize="9" color="#aaaaaa">Campaign, Time Attack, Zen, Daily, Speed Run, Minimal Moves, Random, Practice</text>
  </container>
  <text name="btn-back" fontSize="12" color="#888888" backgroundColor="#222222" padding="5" borderRadius="4" textAlign="center" cursor="pointer" hover-backgroundColor="#444444" width="300">BACK</text>
</container>
","path":"workspace/neon-gravity/ui/help.uikitml"}
